The US overdose deaths data analyzed in this report were acquired from the National Vital Statistics System (NVSS) and downloaded as a .csv file. For every month from January 2015 to October 2025, this dataset captured the number of deaths in US jurisdictions due to drug overdose that occurred in the 12-month period ending in the month indicated. For example, the reported count of January 2015 for a given US jurisdiction represents the reported number of overdose deaths from January 2014 to January 2015.

Of important note, category #3, Synthetic opioids, excl. methadone, classifies overdose deaths attributed to fentanyl and its derivatives. This is the class of opioids tied to the “third wave” of the US opioid overdose crisis and will be of particular interest to this analysis.
Next, for the state-level analysis to be normalized by population, US yearly census data was added to the NVSS dataset. These population data were acquired from United States Census Bureau (2010-2020, 2020-2025) and downloaded as .xlsx files.
After cleaning the population data, it was attatched to the NVSS table. Two additional columns, drugdeathper100k and syntheticdeathper100k were created to capture the number of deaths per 100,000 in the population from all drug overdoses and synthetic opioid overdosees, respectively.
Finally, the NVSS dataset was filtered into two separate data sets based on the State variable, to separate US vs state-level data.

title: 620 Final Report
text:
Results

National Trends








Figure 1 plots the 12 month-ending reported counts of drug overdose deaths in the United States from January 2015 to October 2025 for all drugs, synthetic opioids, natural and semi-synthetic opioids, and heroin. To reiterate interpretation once more, the reported drug overdose count data of January 2015 represents the total reported number of drug overdose deaths from January 2014 to January 2015 in the US.
Most notably, this figure reveals that from the end of 2016 onwards, out of the three opioid categories, synthetic opioid overdoses contribute the largest proportion of deaths to the total 12 month-ending reported overdose death counts. The trend of the total 12 month-ending drug overdose deaths also closely mirrors the trend of 12 month-ending synthetic opioid deaths. Both curves show a sharp increase in 12 month-ending deaths from 2019 to 2023, and similarly decrease from 2023 to 2025. The sharp increase in overdose deaths from 2019 to 2023 also aligns with the timeframe of the COVID-19 Pandemic.
The maximum 12 month-ending reported total overdose death count (111,466) and synthetic opioid overdose death count (77,695) were reported in June 2023.
This contrasts the trend of 12 month-ending reported counts of heroin deaths, which is higher than that of synthetic opioids in 2015, but plateaus and then begins decreasing in 2020. The trend of 12 month-ending reported overdose deaths from natural and semi-synthetic opioids follows a very similar pattern.
That being said, it is important to recognize that the reported counts of overdose deaths (overall, and in each drug category) can be lower than the true counts [see Data Collection Process]. Furthermore, there is also missing data from certain states at certain time points regarding the specific cause-of-death drug category [see Missing Data]. Thus, the trendlines for synthetic, natural and semi-synthetic, and heroin overdose deaths in this plot underestimate the true counts. However, the overall trends illuminated in this figure do provide a strong picture of the relationship between overall overdose deaths and specific causes of death, such as deaths from synthetic opioids.
The general trends of Figure 1 match general reporting on Wave 3 of the US opioid epidemic, when synthetic opioids like fentanyl exploded in the illicit drug market around 2013. While overdose deaths from heroin and natural opioids wane, synthetic opioids become positioned as the strongest driver of new overdose deaths, clearly defined at the center of this modern public health crisis.


State-Level Trends
Due to the incomplete data on deaths due to specific opioid categories, as seen in the Missing Data Analysis, this section of analysis will study overdose death trends between different states by focusing on the 12 month-ending reported counts of drug overdose deaths data.








The 12 month-ending reported drug overdose deaths in each US state per 100,000 state residents are visualized in Figure 2. Unlike Figure 1, which showed a strong relationship between new drug overdose deaths and time, the most defining pattern that emerges from this heatmap isn’t related to time, but individual states.
Generally, the heatmap does reveal that many states experienced an increase in 12 month-ending drug overdose deaths per 100,000 between 2020 and 2023, shown by the lighter “block” in the upper half of the heatmap. This follows trends from Figure 1, where drug overdose deaths sharply increased from 2019 to 2023.
However, the most distinct pattern of this heatmap highlights that across the decade, the District of Columbia and West Virginia consistently had significantly higher 12 month-ending overdose deaths per 100,000 compared to other US states. The number of 12 month-ending overdose deaths peaks for the District of Columbia at 94 per 100,000 in September 2023 and for West Virginia at 88 per 100,000 in April 2021.








Figure 3 explores the percent change for each US state in 12 month-ending drug overdose deaths per 100,000, comparing data from January 2015 and October 2025. This figure further highlights that the degree of severity of the modern overdose crisis varies greatly between states; there is no singular trend that every state follows.
For example, some states, like Michigan and New Hampshire, saw a decrease in 12 month-ending drug overdose deaths per 100,000 (-11.11% and -30.77%, respectively) when comparing reported counts from January 2015 and October 2025.
However, many more states experienced percent increases in 12 month-ending drug overdose deaths per 100,000. Overall, states on the west side of the country appeared to experience larger percentage increases in overdose deaths compared to the east side. Of particular concern are Washington and Alaska, which experienced some of the largest percent increase in 12 month-ending drug overdose deaths across the decade (178% and 159%, respectively).



Table 1: Percent Change in 12 Month-Ending Drug Overdose Deaths
(per 100,000) in US States, comparing January 2015 and October 2025





Only eight states experienced decreases in 12 month-ending drug overdose deaths per 100,000 when comparing January 2015 and October 2025 data. New Hampshire and Wyoming saw the greatest decrease (-31% and -28%, respectively).
On the other hand, 41 states experienced percent increases in 12 month-ending drug overdose deaths per 100,000. Washington, the District of Columbia, and Alaska saw the greatest percent increases (179%, 176%, and 159%, respectively).
The minimum, mean, and maximum percent change in 12 month-ending drug overdose deaths per 100,000 were -31%, 41%, and 179%—highlighting the expansive range and variation in trends among the US states.
That being said, there are some limitations with Figure 3 and Table 1. As seen in Figure 1, US overdose death trends revealed that 2023 was the first year since 2015 to see a decrease in 12 month-ending drug overdose deaths. However, Figure 3 masks this trend by just capturing snapshots in 2015 and 2025. Thus, an additional subanalysis will now explore the percent change in 12 month-ending drug overdose deaths in US states before and after 2023.
















Using 2023 as the critical inflection point, Figure 4a visualizes the percent change for each US state in 12 month-ending drug overdose deaths per 100,000, comparing January 2015 and January 2023 data. Figure 4b covers the same percent change between January 2023 and October 2025.
Figure 4a unveils some of the trends missed in Figure 3. Between 2015 and 2023, Utah was the only state to see a decrease in 12 month-ending drug overdose deaths; many more states experienced an overwhelming increase in 12 month-ending drug overdose deaths.
Figure 4b is also revealing. Between 2023 and 2025, a majority of states saw a decrease in 12 month-ending drug overdose deaths. The few states that still saw increases in 12 month-ending overdose deaths were concentrated on the west side of the country.
This pair of figures helps unpack Figure 3, and shows that while overdose deaths were rising quite dramatically up until around 2023, efforts to decrease deaths have been successful and resulted in decreases of 12 month-ending overdose deaths in US states.



Table 2: Percent Change in 12 Month-Ending Drug Overdose Deaths
(per 100,000) in US States, comparing 2015-2023 and 2023-2025





Comparing January 2015 to January 2023 data, the District of Columbia, Vermont, and Maine saw the largest percent increases in 12 month-ending drug overdose deaths per 100,000 (394%, 242%, and 219%, respectively). All states other than Utah saw percent increases in deaths. The minimum, mean, and maximum of the percent changes per 100,000 were -9.5%, 117%, and 394%. Overall, the percent increases of 12 month-ending drug overdose deaths in US states during this timeframe are also much larger in magnitude than those seen comparing January 2015 to October 2025 data in Table 1.
When comparing January 2023 to October 2025 data, the opposite trend is seen: a majority of states experienced percent decreases in 12 month-ending drug overdose deaths per 100,000. Only five states saw a percent increase in death counts during this time: Alaska, Hawaii, Washington, Nevada, and Arizona. North Carolina and Rhode Island saw the greatest decreases in magnitude, with -59% and -55%, respectively. Notably, Vermont experienced the 7th greatest decrease in magnitude (-51%). The minimum, mean, and maximum of the percent changes per 100,000 were -59%, -33%, and 19%.


State Specific Trends
To more closely hone-in on distinct state trends, this section will focus on the unique drug overdose death trends of three states identified from the State-Level Trend Results:

District of Columbia: The District of Columbia saw the highest reported count of 12 month-ending drug overdose deaths per 100,000 among any US state across the decade [Figure 2]. This district also experienced the greatest percent increase in 12 month-ending drug overdose deaths per 100,000, comparing January 2015 to January 2023 [Table 2].
Washington: Washington saw the greatest percent increase in 12 month-ending drug overdose deaths per 100,000 between January 2015 and October 2025 [Table 1]. Furthermore, it was one of only five states to still see a percent increase in 12 month-ending overdose deaths per 100,000 between January 2023 and October 2025 [Table 2].
Vermont: Vermont experienced the second-highest percent increase in 12 month-ending overdose deaths per 100,000 between January 2015 and January 2023 [Table 2]. However, it also experienced the seventh greatest percent decrease in 12 month-ending overdose deaths per 100,000 between January 2023 and October 2025 [Table 2].









Across the decade, Figure 5 clearly visualizes the distinct differences in the trends of 12 month-ending overdose deaths per 100,000 between these three states/districts.
The District of Columbia consistently had higher 12 month-ending overdose death counts compared to Washington and Vermont. While reported counts rose rapidly from 2019 to 2023, the number of reported 12 month-ending overdose deaths also sharply decreased from September 2023 onwards (generally matching national trends).
Washington began January 2015 with a low reported count of 12 month-ending overdose deaths, but those numbers began steadily rising starting from 2020 onwards. The reported counts of Washington’s 12 month-ending overdose deaths decreased slightly and plateaued at the start of 2024, but saw nowhere near the decrease of that of the District of Columbia (or Vermont).
Vermont, like Washington, began the decade with the lowest reported count of 12 month-ending overdose deaths out of the three states, only for those counts to sharply increase from 2020 to 2023. However, unlike Washington, Vermont also experienced a very sharp decrease in 12 month-ending overdose deaths from April 2023 onwards.
Because these three states all have complete reported data for overdose deaths attributed to synthetic opioids, Figures 6 compares the total 12 month-ending drug overdose deaths to 12 month-ending synthetic opioid overdose deaths in the different states.








Though the trends of 12 month-ending overdose deaths per 100,000 between the District of Columbia, Vermont, and Washington are very different, Figures 6 does reveals one striking similarity: deaths from synthetic opioid overdoses appear to be a very significant driver of the overall 12 month-ending overdose deaths trends in these three jursidictions.
Similar to Figure 1, the trendlines of the 12 month-ending overdose deaths very closely match the trendline shapes of the 12 month-ending synthetic opioid overdose deaths. Furthermore, starting around 2018 (2022 for Washington), for each month in these states, the 12 month-ending reported overdose deaths from synthetic opioids make up a clear majority of the total 12 month-ending reported drug overdose deaths.

title: 620 Final Report
text:
Conclusion
Using NVSS data that captured the 12 month-ending reported drug overdose deaths of US jurisdictions from January 2015 to October 2025, the primary objective of this analysis was to study trends of synthetic opioid overdose deaths and overall drug overdose deaths across the decade, at the national and state level. Secondary analysis focused on specific states with unique overdose trends.
Overall, this analysis revealed that during this decade, synthetic opioid overdose deaths were a large contributor to total overdose deaths in 12 month periods in the United States. Overdose deaths from all drugs as well as synthetic opioids in 12 month periods increased from 2015 to 2023, with a notably rapid increase between 2020 and 2023 [Figure 1]. This aligns with other research that has noted the 2020 COVID-19 Pandemic’s negative impact on the overdose crisis [1]. However, towards the latter half of 2023, overdose deaths from all drugs and synthetic opioids in 12 month periods began steadily decreasing [Figure 1].
While analysis at the national level revealed a general relationship between the progression of time and the number of reported drug overdoses in 12 month periods, there was still much inter-state variability in these trends. Some states experienced large percent increases in 12 month-ending drug overdose deaths between January 2015 and October 2025, while others experienced notable decreases [Figure 3]. Furthermore, the magnitudes of the percent increases / decreases were quite varied as well. When broken up by percent changes in 12 month-ending drug overdose deaths per state before and after January 2023, similar variation was observed [Figure 4a and Figure 4b].
Finally, specific analysis of three US jurisdictions further highlighted varying trends in 12 month-ending drug overdose deaths across the decade, but all showed a strong relationship between total overdose deaths and synthetic opioid overdose deaths. In the latter half of the decade, as 12 month-ending synthetic opioid overdose deaths increased, so did the 12 month-ending drug overdose deaths, and vice-versa for decreases [Figure 6].
The major limitation of this study comes from the nature of reported mortality data for drug overdoses and the variance in reporting procedures in different states [see Data Collection Process]. Furthermore, as indicated by the Missing Data tab, there were states with missing data for the number of reported deaths attributed to specific opioid overdoses in 12 month periods. Thus, for example, Figure 1 underrepresents the true counts of overdose deaths per 12 month periods from the specific opioid classes. Furthermore, the primary “unit” of data, deaths in 12 month-ending periods, is inherently a less intuitive measurement (than, say incident deaths per month). Yet, despite these limtations, these data do help illustrate overall trends that are useful for hypothesis generation and policy development.
With a strong understanding of how tied the United States’ drug overdose crisis is to synthetic opioids overdoses, it is worth qualitatively considering the trends seen in the District of Columbia and Vermont, which can illuminate what policies may be consequential in synthetic opioid overdose intervention. Both jurisdictions saw rapid increases in synthetic opioid (and total) drug overdose deaths prior to 2023, but also experienced sharp decreases in reported overdose death counts from 2023 onward [Figure 6 ].
In the District of Columbia, government officials focused on “building a strong, responsive system of care for residents…..distributing over 100,000 free naloxone kits in 2024 and opening the D.C. Stabilization Center—a 24/7 facility providing immediate care for individuals experiencing acute intoxication” [2]. Naloxone is a medication that can be administered to an individual experiencing opioid overdose and rapidly reverse the overdose [3]. As of March 2025, “the center has had over 8,200 admissions since opening in 2023 and offers behavioral health support, peer counseling, and links to long-term treatment.” [2]
Similar initiatives have taken place in Vermont. As of December 2023, some critical initiatives the state has instituted include increased naloxone access, which “authorizes pharmacies and other providers to dispense it to anyone so long as the individual reviews guidelines for its administration and either uses drugs or interacts with people who use drugs,” instead of requiring a prescription. Given fentanyl overdoses often occur unknowingly because it is laced in other drugs, “fentanyl test strips are now widely available at community health centers and places that dispense Naloxone.” Furthermore, Vermont has expanded the number of medically assisted treatment programs that use buprenorphine to help opioid users in recovery [4].
It is also important to recognize work being done at the federal level to fight the modern opioid epidemic. Since 2020, the Opioid Rapid Response Program (ORRP) has been a program formally managed by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) to reduce opioid deaths. The ORRP “enhance[s] rapid response and risk mitigation when disruptions occur that have the potential to increase overdose risk among affected patients” [5]. In March 2023, the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) also approved the first over-the-counter Naloxone nasal spray, which can be administered without medical training to reduce opioid deaths [6].
Though it may be too soon to fully evaluate the impact of these overdose prevention strategies at the state and federal level, it is clear government systems are intentionally addressing this crisis with meaningful policy and programs. There is, of course, still work to be done: at the national level, the 12 month-ending drug overdose reported death count was still higher in October 2025 than in January 2015.
As policymakers and government leaders continue to work on reducing synthetic opioid overdose deaths in communities, future research could focus on evaluating programs in jurisdictions like the District of Columbia and Vermont (where decreases in overdose deaths have been observed) and on how to implement such initiatives in other US states.

[1] https://www.sph.umn.edu/news/u-s-opioid-crisis-worsened-during-covid-19-pandemic/
[2] https://publichealth.jhu.edu/2025/experts-officials-convene-in-dc-to-discuss-nations-nonfatal-overdose-crisis
[3] https://nida.nih.gov/publications/drugfacts/naloxone
[4] https://www.uvm.edu/d10-files/documents/2024-06/The-State-of-the-Opiate-Crisis-in-Vermont.pdf
[5] https://www.cdc.gov/overdose-prevention/orrp/index.html
[6] https://www.fda.gov/drugs/postmarket-drug-safety-information-patients-and-providers/information-about-naloxone-and-nalmefene

title: Analysis of US Drug Overdose Death Trends between 2015 and 2025
text:
Since the 1990s, one looming facet of the United States’ challenge with rising drug overdose deaths has been characterized by the opioid overdose epidemic.
Opioids are a class of natural and synthetic substances that block pain signals in the body. It is common practice to use opioids to treat pain in medical settings. However, given their desirable pain relieving quantities, opioids are also often subject to misuse/abuse—and when taken in large quantities, opioids can decrease respiration and lead to death by oxygen deprivation to the body’s organs [1].





The opioid overdose public health crisis is classically defined by public health researchers in three waves [2]:

Wave 1 [1990s]: First prominent rise in opioid overdose deaths, largely attributed to the increased prescription of opioids by medical professionals.
Wave 2 [2010 - 2015]: Rise in overdose deaths related to heroin, a stronger synthetic opioid than traditional prescription opioids.
Wave 3 [2013 - Present]: Defined by the widespread emergence of fentanyl, an extremely potent synthetic opioid derivative, on the illicit drug market. Fentanyl and its derivatives are very cheap to manufacture, and are often also laced in other illicit drugs (cocaine, methamphetamine, etc).

The National Vital Statistics System (NVSS) is an inter-governmental system for sharing US vital data (births, deaths, marriages, etc). The US drug overdose death dataset used in this analysis was produced by the NVSS, and reports the number of drug overdose deaths in 12-month periods for US jurisdictions from 2015 to 2025.
The primary goal of this analysis is to study the trends of overall drug overdose deaths and synthetic opioid overdose deaths from 2015 to 2025, at the national and state level. Secondary analysis will examine specific jurisdictions with unique drug overdose death trends.

REPORT ABOUT THE DATA


[1] https://nida.nih.gov/research-topics/opioids
[2] https://www.cdc.gov/overdose-prevention/about/understanding-the-opioid-overdose-epidemic.html

text:

title: About the Data
text:
The data analyzed in this study comes from reported drug overdose mortality data monitored by the United States National Vital Statistics System (NVSS). The NVSS is an inter-governmental system designed to share data on vital statistics (births, deaths, marriages, etc) of jurisdictions of the United States.
Reported provisional counts in the dataset indicate the number of deaths due to drug overdose that occurred in the 12-month period ending of the month indicated. For example, the reported count of January 2015 for a given US jurisdiction represents the reported number of overdose deaths from January 2014 to January 2015.
Reported counts were available for total overdose deaths and drug-specific overdose deaths, at the national and state-level. Deaths were reported for the US jurisdiction in which the death occurred.
Additional documentation from NVSS can be found here.


There is typically a lag time between when deaths occurred and when the mortality cause of death data in the NVSS surveillance database updated. Drug overdose deaths are often reported initially without a cause of death because toxicology testing can sometimes be a lengthy process. For drug overdose-related deaths, the latent period between death and reporting to NVSS is typically 4-6 months*.
It is also important to recognize that these data are often incomplete, and the degree of completeness varies by each US jurisdiction. Thus, the number of drug overdose deaths are often underestimated based on reported data relative to the true counts, and are subject to random variation.
That being said, these data are still very valuable; the goal of analyzing these data is surveillance and monitoring of emerging public health trends.
*For this report (conducted April 2026), the last month with reported drug overdose deaths data in the NVSS dataset was October 2025. Based on the data documentation, it was deemed appropriate to include all the data from January 2015 up to October 2025 in the analysis.



To address discrepancies between reported and true counts, methods were developed by NVSS to adjust reported counts for reporting delays by generating a set of predicted counts in the dataset.
NVSS data documentation states, “State predicted drug overdose death counts differed by an average of 0.1% (ranging from 0.0 to 0.5%). National predicted counts for deaths involving opioids had a difference of 0.1%, while state predicted counts had an average difference of 0.3% (ranging from 0.3 to 2.9%).”
Thus, given that the primary goal of this analysis was to explore overall US drug overdose death trends over time (2015-2025) and the percent differences between reported and predicted values were small (compared to the percent changes in counts over time, as seen in the report), the decision was made to focus the report’s analysis on the reported counts in the dataset.



The US overdose deaths data were acquired from the National Vital Statistics System (NVSS) and downloaded as a .csv file.
These eight packages were used in the data cleaning, wrangling, and visualization steps of the report:

library(tidyverse)
library(readr)

Inspecting the unique US jurisdictions under the State column revealed four non-traditional categories: Puerto Rico, United States, District of Columbia, and New York City.
Given the NVSS documentation, “the total death count for the United States does not include deaths occurring in Puerto Rico,” it was decided to exclude Puerto Rico data from this analysis.
Data for New York City and New York were combined into one category, New York. Data corresponding to the District of Columbia was maintained in the dataset, given that these data did contribute to the national overdose death totals.


Code
# The total death count for the United States does not include deaths occurring in Puerto Rico.
drug_deaths <- drug_deaths %>% 
  filter(!State == "Puerto Rico")

# Combine New York City and New York together:
new_york <- drug_deaths %>% 
  filter(State == "New York" | State == "New York City")
new_york$State <- NULL

new_york <- new_york %>% pivot_wider(names_from = State.Abb,
                                      values_from = Deaths) %>%
  mutate(Deaths = NY + YC)
new_york <- new_york %>% 
  select(Month, Year, Date, Indicator, Deaths) %>% 
  mutate(State.Abb = rep("NY", dim(new_york)[1]),
         State = rep("New York", dim(new_york)[1])) %>% 
  relocate(c(State.Abb, State), .before = Month) 

# Add in "New York" (state) values back to dataset
drug_deaths <- drug_deaths %>% 
  filter(State != "New York") %>% 
    filter(State != "New York City") 
drug_deaths <- rbind(drug_deaths, new_york)

Of the available overdose death data available in the dataset, this analysis focused on four categories that captured yearly counts of overdose deaths related to different opioid classes [1]:

Heroin (T40.1)
Natural & semi-synthetic opioids (T40.2)
Synthetic opioids, excl. methadone (T40.4)
Number of Drug Overdose Deaths [to serve as a comparison]

Heroin, natural & semi-synthetic opioids, and synthetic opioids were selected for this analysis because of their corresponding significance to different “waves” of the US opioid overdose epidemic.
The opioid overdose crisis began in the 1990s with the overprescription of “prescription opioids,” such as hydrocodone, oxycodone, and morphine. These prescription opioids belong to the class natural & semi-synthetic opioids, which are derived and modified compounds originating from the opium poppy plant.
The second wave of the opioid epidemic began in 2010 when deaths attributed to heroin overdoses started rapidly increasing. Heroin is a highly addictive derivative of morphine.
The third wave of the crisis is defined by the emergence and saturation of synthetic opioids, like fentanyl and tramadol, in the illegal drug market. These synthetic opioids are extremely potent and subject to high overdose risk.
Deaths due to each of these opioids are associated with an International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems (ICD) Code. ICD provides standardized guidance used in almost every country to code and classify causes of death. Thus, deaths due to heroin overdose are categorized as T40.1, natural & semi-synthetic opioids deaths as T40.2, etc.
Deaths where “opioid” was reported as the cause without more specific information were assigned the general ICD-10 code T40.0–T40.4. These were not included in the analysis given the lack of specificity.
As expected, drug overdose deaths can also involve multiple drugs. Following ICD standards, such a death may be included in more than one “cause of death” category. For example, an overdose death involving heroin and fentanyl would be coded as T40.1 and T40.4, thus appearing as a count in both categorizations.
pivot_wider() was then used to create column headers defined by the causes of death.


Code
# Filter to indicators of interest
drug_deaths <- drug_deaths %>% 
  filter(Indicator == "Heroin (T40.1)" | 
           Indicator == "Number of Drug Overdose Deaths" |
           Indicator == "Natural & semi-synthetic opioids (T40.2)" |
           Indicator == "Synthetic opioids, excl. methadone (T40.4)") %>% 
  mutate(Indicator = case_when(
    Indicator == "Heroin (T40.1)" ~ "Heroin",
    Indicator == "Number of Drug Overdose Deaths" ~ "Drug.Overdose",
    Indicator == "Natural & semi-synthetic opioids (T40.2)" ~ "Natural.SemiSynthetic",
    TRUE ~ "Synthetic"
  ))

drug_deaths$Indicator <- with(drug_deaths, 
                              factor(Indicator, 
                                     levels = c("Heroin", "Natural.SemiSynthetic",
                                                "Synthetic",
                                                "Drug.Overdose")))

drug_deaths <- drug_deaths %>% 
  pivot_wider(names_from = Indicator,
              values_from = Deaths)

To format both datasets, the column names needed to be adjusted to properly reflect the years that corresponded with the data. Certain rows also needed to be de-selected to properly align the data. gsub() was then used to format the state names. Finally, pivot_longer() was used to turn the dataframe into tidy data (so that each variable is a column, each row is a single observation, and each cell is a single value).


Code
# Population of 2010s
pop_2010s <- pop_2010s %>% select(-2)
colnames(pop_2010s) <- c("State", seq(2010, 2020, 1))
pop_2010s <- pop_2010s[-c(1:3, 5:8, 60:67),]
pop_2010s$State <- gsub("\\.", "", pop_2010s$State)
pop_2010s <- pop_2010s %>% 
  mutate(across(`2010`:`2020`, as.numeric)) %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols = `2010`:`2020`,
    names_to = "Year",
    values_to = "Population")

# Population of 2020s
pop_2020s <- readxl::read_xlsx("pop_2020.xlsx")
pop_2020s <- pop_2020s %>% select(-c(2:3))
colnames(pop_2020s) <- c("State", seq(2021, 2025, 1))
pop_2020s <- pop_2020s[-c(1:3, 5:8, 60:67),]
pop_2020s$State <- gsub("\\.", "", pop_2020s$State)
pop_2020s <- pop_2020s %>% 
  mutate(across(`2021`:`2025`, as.numeric)) %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols = `2021`:`2025`,
    names_to = "Year",
    values_to = "Population")


Both datasets were stacked together using cbind() to hold census data from 2010-2025, and left_join() was used to merge the population data to the NVSS table.
Two additional columns, drugdeathper100k and syntheticdeathper100k were created using mutate() to capture the number of deaths per 100,000 per year in the population from all drug overdose deaths and synthetic opioids, respectively.


Code
pop <- rbind(pop_2010s, pop_2020s) %>% 
  mutate(Year = as.numeric(Year))

# Adding Population to drug_deaths
drug_deaths <- left_join(drug_deaths, pop, 
          by = c("State", "Year"))

# Creating "drugdeathper100k"
drug_deaths <- drug_deaths %>% 
  mutate(drugdeathper100k = round((Drug.Overdose/Population)*100000)) %>% 
  mutate(syntheticdeathper100k = round((Synthetic/Population)*100000))

Natural, semi-synthetic, & synthetic opioids, incl. methadone (T40.2-T40.4)

Of the available overdose death data available in the dataset, this analysis focused on four categories that captured yearly counts of overdose deaths related to different opioid classes [1]:

Heroin (T40.1)
Natural & semi-synthetic opioids (T40.2)
Synthetic opioids, excl. methadone (T40.4)
Number of Drug Overdose Deaths [to serve as a comparison]

Heroin, natural & semi-synthetic opioids, and synthetic opioids were selected for this analysis because of their corresponding significance to different “waves” of the US opioid overdose epidemic.
The opioid overdose crisis began in the 1990s with the overprescription of “prescription opioids,” such as hydrocodone, oxycodone, and morphine. These prescription opioids belong to the class natural & semi-synthetic opioids, which are derived and modified compounds originating from the opium poppy plant.
The second wave of the opioid epidemic began in 2010 when deaths attributed to heroin overdoses started rapidly increasing. Heroin is a highly addictive derivative of morphine.
The third wave of the crisis is defined by the emergence and saturation of synthetic opioids, like fentanyl and tramadol, in the illegal drug market. These synthetic opioids are extremely potent and subject to high overdose risk.
Deaths due to each of these opioids are associated with an International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems (ICD) Code. ICD provides standardized guidance used in almost every country to code and classify causes of death. Thus, deaths due to heroin overdose are categorized as T40.1, natural & semi-synthetic opioids deaths as T40.2, etc.
Deaths where “opioid” was reported as the cause without more specific information were assigned the general ICD-10 code T40.0–T40.4. These were not included in the analysis given the lack of specificity.
As expected, drug overdose deaths can also involve multiple drugs. Following ICD standards, such a death may be included in more than one “cause of death” category. For example, an overdose death involving heroin and fentanyl would be coded as T40.1 and T40.4, thus appearing as a count in both categorizations.
pivot_wider() was then used to create column headers defined by the causes of death.


Code
# Filter to indicators of interest
drug_deaths <- drug_deaths %>% 
  filter(Indicator == "Heroin (T40.1)" | 
           Indicator == "Number of Drug Overdose Deaths" |
           Indicator == "Natural & semi-synthetic opioids (T40.2)" |
           Indicator == "Synthetic opioids, excl. methadone (T40.4)") %>% 
  mutate(Indicator = case_when(
    Indicator == "Heroin (T40.1)" ~ "Heroin",
    Indicator == "Number of Drug Overdose Deaths" ~ "Drug.Overdose",
    Indicator == "Natural & semi-synthetic opioids (T40.2)" ~ "Natural.SemiSynthetic",
    TRUE ~ "Synthetic"
  ))

drug_deaths$Indicator <- with(drug_deaths, 
                              factor(Indicator, 
                                     levels = c("Heroin", "Natural.SemiSynthetic",
                                                "Synthetic",
                                                "Drug.Overdose")))

drug_deaths <- drug_deaths %>% 
  pivot_wider(names_from = Indicator,
              values_from = Deaths)

To format both datasets, the column names needed to be adjusted to properly reflect the years that corresponded with the data. Certain rows also needed to be de-selected to properly align the data. gsub() was then used to format the state names. Finally, pivot_longer() was used to turn the dataframe into tidy data (so that each variable is a column, each row is a single observation, and each cell is a single value).


Code
# Population of 2010s
pop_2010s <- pop_2010s %>% select(-2)
colnames(pop_2010s) <- c("State", seq(2010, 2020, 1))
pop_2010s <- pop_2010s[-c(1:3, 5:8, 60:67),]
pop_2010s$State <- gsub("\\.", "", pop_2010s$State)
pop_2010s <- pop_2010s %>% 
  mutate(across(`2010`:`2020`, as.numeric)) %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols = `2010`:`2020`,
    names_to = "Year",
    values_to = "Population")

# Population of 2020s
pop_2020s <- readxl::read_xlsx("pop_2020.xlsx")
pop_2020s <- pop_2020s %>% select(-c(2:3))
colnames(pop_2020s) <- c("State", seq(2021, 2025, 1))
pop_2020s <- pop_2020s[-c(1:3, 5:8, 60:67),]
pop_2020s$State <- gsub("\\.", "", pop_2020s$State)
pop_2020s <- pop_2020s %>% 
  mutate(across(`2021`:`2025`, as.numeric)) %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols = `2021`:`2025`,
    names_to = "Year",
    values_to = "Population")


Both datasets were stacked together using cbind() to hold census data from 2010-2025, and left_join() was used to merge the population data to the NVSS table.
Two additional columns, drugdeathper100k and syntheticdeathper100k were created using mutate() to capture the number of deaths per 100,000 per year in the population from all drug overdose deaths and synthetic opioids, respectively.


Code
pop <- rbind(pop_2010s, pop_2020s) %>% 
  mutate(Year = as.numeric(Year))

# Adding Population to drug_deaths
drug_deaths <- left_join(drug_deaths, pop, 
          by = c("State", "Year"))

# Creating "drugdeathper100k"
drug_deaths <- drug_deaths %>% 
  mutate(drugdeathper100k = round((Drug.Overdose/Population)*100000)) %>% 
  mutate(syntheticdeathper100k = round((Synthetic/Population)*100000))

For standardization purposes, the population used in the “per 100,000” calculations is the population of the year that the 12-month period ended in.
For example, between April 2022 and April 2023, the 7743 drug overdose deaths per 100,000 occurred in Florida. To compute this observation’s corresponding drugdeathper100k value, Florida’s recorded population size in 2023 was used in the denominator of the calculation.
Though this is admittedly not a perfect method, it nonetheless provides a reasonable estimate of 12-month period overdose deaths per 100,000 that can be used to follow trends over time, aligning with the goals of this analysis.

The other variables of interest in this analysis are:

Heroin: Reported counts of heroin overdose deaths during the 12 month-ending period.
Natural.SemiSynthetic: Reported counts of overdose deaths from natural or semi-synthetic opioids during the 12 month-ending period.
Synthetic: Reported counts of overdose deaths from synthetic opioids during the 12 month-ending period.
Drug.Overdose: Reported counts of drug overdose deaths during the 12 month-ending period.
drugdeathper100k: Reported counts of drug overdose deaths during the 12 month-ending period per 100,000 (based on the yearly population of the last month in the 12 month period).
syntheticdeathper100k: Reported counts of overdose deaths from synthetic opioids during the 12 month-ending period per 100,000 (based on the yearly population of the last month in the 12 month period).

Louisiana and Pennsylvania are the only two states with no Heroin, Natural.SemiSynthetic, nor Synthetic data. Drug overdose deaths were reported in these states, but those deaths were not categorized by their causes. Thirteen states have complete data for Natural.SemiSynthetic and Synthetic. Deaths from heroin appear to be the most under-reported in states. It is also possible that certain states in certain years may have categorized opioid overdose deaths as general Opioid (T40.0-T40.4, T40.6) deaths, instead of the specific opioid class that caused the overdose death.
There is also a time component that would be considered. Over the decade, detection and reporting systems may have improved to better detect specific drugs attributed to overdose cause(s) of death.









As hypothesized, the proportion of missing data for the drug categories per year does decrease over time, though a small increase is seen from 2023-2025. Similar to what was seen at the in Table 3, the drug category with the largest proportion of missing data is consistently heroin. Again, there is no missing drug overdose death data in any year.
Based on NVSS documentation and this missing data analysis, reporting of drug overdose deaths appears to be the most consistent between different US states. Overdose deaths are typically reported first and more consistently before cause of death is finalized, given investigating the direct cause of death is often a more involved process and different states have different investigation processes/resource delegation for investigation. Thus, the primary focus of this analysis will focus on analyzing drug overdose data.


Exploratory Data Analysis
To first begin exploring the NVSS data, the national number of reported drug overdose deaths per 100,000 in 12 month-ending periods were plotted against the year the period ended in.









The years with the greatest variation (range & interquartile range) in national 12 month-ending reported drug overdose deaths were 2020 and 2024. Furthermore, these boxplots show generally show the median 12 month-ending reported drug overdose deaths per year increasing from 2015 to 2023, and then beginning to decrease from 2023 to 2025. This begins to suggest a relationship of drug overdose deaths and time.
Next, the same analysis was conducted with data from the state-level dataset.









Though not completely surprising, it is noteworthy how different Figures 2 and 3 look. The variation in 12 month-ending reported drug overdose deaths at the state level per year is much greater than at the national level. Though a general trend can be seen through time (similar to that of Figure 2, with yearly median 12 month-ending reported drug overdose deaths increasing before 2023 and decrease after), it is somewhat masked by how large the ranges are of 12 month-ending reported drug overdose deaths in each year—especially between 2020 to 2024. This begins to suggest large variation in incident drug overdose deaths by state, something the report will further explore.
To further explore state variation, the ranges of the 12 month-ending reported drug overdose deaths for each state were plotted for all the available state data.









Now, the variation of state data becomes even more apparent. Though time is definitely a component of the variation that is not captured in this figure, these marginal distributions provide a strong glimpse of how varied the 12 month-ending reported drug overdoses counts are per state.
For example, the District of Columbia has the largest range of 12 month-ending reported drug overdoses counts per 100,000 and the largest 12 month-ending reported drug overdoses of any month in any year (>75). To compare, South Dakota has a significantly smaller range of 12 month-ending reported drug overdoses counts per 100,000 and saw a maximum 12 month-ending reported drug overdoses counts per 100,000 that was <10 in all the months of collected data.
